planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown shrubs in very well-drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Jerusalem sage requires excellent drainage and dislikes wet soils especially in winter. It is damaged below about 10-15 degrees F and is best suited to zones 7-10. Hard-prune in spring to prevent legginess and maintain a compact shrub form.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun; Jerusalem sage is a robust, aromatic Mediterranean shrub producing some of the most distinctive and beautiful yellow flowers in the ornamental garden. The large, rounded leaves are covered in dense, soft, white-woolly hairs that give the entire plant a silvery-gray appearance and make it exceptionally drought-tolerant. In late spring and early summer, tall stems bear whorled clusters of vivid, brilliant-yellow tubular flowers arranged in stacked rings around the stem in tiers, giving a candelabra effect that is unique among flowering shrubs. The dried brown seed whorls persist attractively on the stems through winter and are ornamental in their own right. Native to rocky, dry hillsides throughout the eastern Mediterranean from Greece to Israel, it thrives in the hot, dry, alkaline conditions of Mediterranean-climate gardens, Gulf Coast landscapes, and dry borders where most ornamental shrubs would struggle. Combines beautifully with lavender, rosemary, and other silver-leafed Mediterranean plants.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or plant container-grown plants in well-drained to moderately moist, neutral to alkaline soil; Turkish sage is a vigorous spreader by rhizomes and will fill in a large area over several years. Unlike shrubby Jerusalem sage, this is a herbaceous perennial that dies back to the ground in winter. It tolerates partial shade and poorer soils better than most Phlomis.

directions: Plant in well-drained to moderately moist, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to part shade; Turkish sage is a herbaceous perennial from Turkey and Syria with one of the most dramatic architectural presences of any medium-sized perennial. The large, broadly heart-shaped, soft, sage-textured leaves form a ground-covering rosette that spreads steadily by rhizomes to fill a substantial area, effectively suppressing weeds with bold foliage. In early summer, stout, upright flower stalks rise 3-4 feet above the foliage bearing the distinctive whorled, tiered clusters of soft-yellow flowers arranged in evenly spaced rings around the stem — an unusual and eye-catching form. After flowering, the persistent, orbicular, pale-buff seed whorls remain erect and attractive through winter, providing exceptional structure and interest in the dormant garden. Unlike the shrubby Jerusalem sage (P. fruticosa), P. russeliana is fully herbaceous and cold-hardy to zone 4, making it adaptable across a much wider range of climates. More shade-tolerant than most Phlomis species.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown shrubs in very well-drained, lean soil in full sun; shrubby germander tolerates pruning well and can be sheared into a formal low hedge or shaped as a specimen. It is damaged below about 15 degrees F and is best suited to zones 8-11. The cultivar Azureum has more intensely blue-lavender flowers than the typical species.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun; shrubby germander is a standout evergreen Mediterranean shrub valued for its exceptional silver-white foliage that glows brilliantly in the sun. The entire plant — stems, leaves, and undersides — is covered in dense, white, woolly hairs that give it an almost luminous silver-white appearance in the garden, making it one of the most silver-foliaged of all hardy shrubs. From spring through summer it produces a long succession of two-lipped, pale-blue to lavender flowers with a prominent lower lip, carried singly in the leaf axils along the branches. Native to the western Mediterranean in Spain, Portugal, and North Africa, it thrives in lean, alkaline, well-drained soils and intense sun and is extremely drought-tolerant once established. It is widely used in Mediterranean-style, California, and Gulf Coast gardens as a specimen shrub, low screening hedge, or silver foliage accent. Responds well to shearing and can be kept as a formal hedge of 3-4 feet.

planting.note: Sow seeds in well-drained, lean to moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; Georgia basil is a naturally drought-tolerant native of the southeastern piedmont and coastal plain. It grows in the thin soils of pine flatwoods, open roadsides, and dry woodland edges and does not require supplemental fertilization. Self-sows in appropriate conditions to maintain a colony.

directions: Sow seeds in well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in part shade to full sun; Georgia basil is a small, aromatic, mint-family native wildflower of the southeastern Piedmont and coastal plain, native to Georgia, Alabama, South Carolina, and northern Florida, growing in pine flatwoods, open roadsides, and dry woodland edges. It produces slender stems with small, oval, hairy, basil-scented leaves and bears clouds of small, two-lipped, pink to lavender-pink flowers throughout summer into fall that attract small native bees, bumblebees, and butterflies. As a genuine southeastern native, it is superbly adapted to the hot, humid summers, periodic drought, and acid sandy soils of the Deep South where non-native mints often struggle. The strong basil-like fragrance from the crushed leaves is pleasant and distinctive. An excellent plant for native gardens, pollinator borders, and naturalized areas in zones 6-9 where it provides season-long bloom with minimal care once established.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring or fall, or root stem cuttings in water; plant in moist, fertile soil in part shade to full sun. Like all mints, bergamot mint spreads vigorously by runners and is best grown in containers or with a root barrier to prevent it from taking over the garden. The distinctive bergamot-citrus fragrance is strongest from midsummer leaves.

directions: Divide runners and plant in moist, fertile soil in part shade to full sun; bergamot mint is a peppermint relative with a distinctive, complex fragrance of bergamot orange, lavender, and lemon — caused by the predominance of linalool and linalool acetate rather than the menthol characteristic of peppermint. The scent is sometimes described as eau de cologne, hence the alternate name. The flavor is equally distinctive and less sharply mentholous than peppermint, making it preferred for herb teas, lemonade, cocktails, and delicate desserts where a floral-citrus mint quality is desired. The leaves are rounded, dark-green to slightly bronze-tinged, and the plant produces small, pale-lavender flower spikes in summer that attract pollinators. Several named cultivars exist including Chocolate Mint (with a chocolate-menthol scent), Orange Mint (with a stronger orange character), and Grapefruit Mint (grapefruit-citrus overtones) — all are selections of this same botanical grouping. Grows identically to spearmint or peppermint in its need for consistent moisture and tendency to spread.
